name: Pre-Calculus
description: Bridges Algebra II and Calculus — covering advanced functions, trigonometry, vectors, parametric equations, polar coordinates, conic sections, and an introduction to limits.

modules.title: Functions: Review & Advanced Concepts
modules.objectives: Review domain and range; compose and invert functions; analyze piecewise functions and transformations. Students will show step-by-step reasoning, check solutions for accuracy, and apply the method to the module assignment or a real problem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 1.1–1.4: Functions, Domain, Range, and Inverses
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Functions, Domain, Range, and Inverse Functions
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: For 5 pairs of functions find f(g(x)) and g(f(x)). Find the inverse of 4 functions and verify by showing f(f⁻¹(x)) = x. Graph 2 piecewise functions and state their domain and range. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Functions: Review & Advanced Concepts module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Polynomial & Rational Functions
modules.objectives: Graph polynomial functions using end behavior and zeros; identify holes versus vertical asymptotes; construct sign charts for rational functions.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 3.3–3.7: Polynomial and Rational Functions
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Graph 4 polynomial functions, identifying all zeros and describing end behavior. Analyze 4 rational functions: find all asymptotes, holes, intercepts, and sketch the graph using a sign chart. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Polynomial & Rational Functions module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Exponential & Logarithmic Functions
modules.objectives: Apply properties of exponents and logarithms; solve exponential and logarithmic equations; model logistic growth; use the change-of-base formula.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 4.1–4.7: Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Solve 8 exponential equations and 8 logarithmic equations. Use change-of-base to evaluate 4 logarithms on a calculator. Write and analyze a logistic growth model for a real-world population scenario. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Exponential & Logarithmic Functions module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Right Triangle Trigonometry
modules.objectives: Apply SOH-CAH-TOA to find missing sides and angles; use special angles (30-60-90, 45-45-90); apply inverse trig functions; solve real-world elevation and depression problems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 5.1–5.2: Right Triangle Trigonometry
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Introduction to Trigonometry and Right Triangles
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Solve 10 right triangles (find all missing sides and angles). Solve 3 angle-of-elevation/depression application problems. Verify the exact values for all special-angle trig ratios from memory. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Right Triangle Trigonometry module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Unit Circle & Radian Measure
modules.objectives: Convert between degrees and radians, locate reference angles, calculate exact trigonometric values from the unit circle, and apply radian measure to solve arc length and sector area problems.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 5.2: Unit Circle, Sine, and Cosine
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Unit Circle and Radian Measure (Full Lecture)
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Convert 10 angles from degrees to radians and 10 from radians to degrees. Fill in all 16 standard unit circle points from memory, then compute arc length and sector area for 4 circles. Submit corrections for any missed unit-circle values with a one-sentence explanation of the quadrant or reference-angle rule used. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Unit Circle & Radian Measure module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Graphs of Trig Functions
modules.objectives: Graph sine, cosine, and tangent functions; identify amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift; write equations from graphs.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 6.1–6.2: Graphs of Sine, Cosine, and Tangent
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.

modules.assignment: Graph 6 sinusoidal functions (identifying amplitude, period, phase shift, and vertical shift). Write equations to match 4 given sinusoidal graphs. Use Desmos to verify all graphs. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Graphs of Trig Functions module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Trig Identities & Equations
modules.objectives: Verify Pythagorean, reciprocal, and quotient identities; apply sum/difference and double-angle formulas; solve trigonometric equations on a given interval.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 7.1–7.3: Trig Identities and Equations

modules.assignment: Verify 6 trig identities algebraically. Use sum/difference formulas to find exact values for 4 non-standard angles. Solve 6 trig equations on [0, 2π). Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Trig Identities & Equations module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. modules.title: Law of Sines & Cosines
modules.objectives: Solve oblique triangles using Law of Sines and Law of Cosines; analyze the ambiguous case (SSA); compute triangle area using Heron's formula and the ½ab sin C formula.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 8.1–8.2: Law of Sines and Law of Cosines
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.

modules.assignment: Solve 6 triangles using Law of Sines and 4 using Law of Cosines. Analyze 3 ambiguous SSA cases — determine how many triangles exist. Compute the area of each solved triangle. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Law of Sines & Cosines module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Vectors & Directional Quantities
modules.objectives: Represent vectors in component form; compute magnitude and direction angle; perform vector addition and scalar multiplication; calculate the dot product and determine orthogonality.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 8.8: Vectors in Two Dimensions
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.video2Note: 3Blue1Brown — Vectors, What Even Are They? (Essence of Linear Algebra)

Compute 4 dot products and determine whether each pair of vectors is orthogonal. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Vectors module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Parametric Equations & Polar Coordinates
modules.objectives: Graph parametric curves by eliminating the parameter, convert between rectangular and polar coordinates, analyze common polar curves, and explain how parameter or angle changes affect the graph.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 8.6–8.7: Parametric Equations and Polar Coordinates
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Graph 4 parametric curves by hand and verify them using Desmos. Convert 6 points from polar to rectangular and 6 from rectangular to polar. Identify and sketch 3 common polar curves (circle, cardioid, rose), then write a short comparison explaining what each equation form makes easier to see. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Parametric Equations & Polar Coordinates module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Conic Sections (Advanced)
modules.objectives: Write and graph conic sections in standard and general form; identify focus, directrix, vertices, and eccentricity for parabolas, ellipses, and hyperbolas; classify conics from the general equation.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 10.3–10.5: Parabola, Ellipse, Hyperbola
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.

modules.assignment: Write equations for 2 parabolas, 2 ellipses, and 2 hyperbolas given key features. Convert 4 conic equations from general form to standard form by completing the square. State focus and eccentricity for each. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Conic Sections (Advanced) module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Sequences, Series & Mathematical Induction
modules.titleZh: 数列、级数与数学归纳法
modules.objectives: Identify and generate arithmetic and geometric sequences; compute partial sums of arithmetic and geometric series; prove statements using mathematical induction. Students will show step-by-step reasoning, check solutions for accuracy, and apply the method to the module assignment or a real problem.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 11.1–11.4: Sequences, Series, and Mathematical Induction
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Find the nth term and the sum of the first 10 terms for 4 arithmetic sequences and 4 geometric sequences. Write and complete 2 proofs by mathematical induction (e.g., sum of first n integers, divisibility). Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Sequences, Series & Mathematical Induction module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Probability & Combinatorics (Advanced)
modules.objectives: Solve counting problems using permutations and combinations, expand binomials with the Binomial Theorem and Pascal's triangle, and calculate theoretical probabilities for multi-step experiments.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 11.5–11.7: Counting, Binomial Theorem, Probability

modules.video2Note: Professor Leonard — Combinatorics and the Binomial Theorem
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Solve 6 permutation and 6 combination problems, labeling when order matters. Expand 4 binomial expressions using the Binomial Theorem, use Pascal's triangle to find a specified term, and solve 3 probability problems involving combinations. For two problems, explain why a permutation or combination model is appropriate. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Probability & Combinatorics (Advanced) module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

modules.title: Introduction to Limits
modules.objectives: Describe the intuitive definition of a limit; evaluate limits graphically and numerically; compute one-sided limits; apply the basic limit laws; determine continuity at a point.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Precalculus 2e — Ch 12.1–12.4: Limits and Continuity
modules.videoNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.video2Note: 3Blue1Brown — Limits, L'Hôpital's Rule, and Epsilon Delta Definitions
modules.practiceNote: GIIS Learn Portal — use the module lesson, assigned course materials, and teacher-reviewed practice inside the GIIS course path; no external practice platform is required.
modules.assignment: Evaluate 8 limits graphically. Compute 8 limits algebraically using limit laws (direct substitution, factoring, rationalizing). Identify 3 points of discontinuity on given graphs and classify each as removable, jump, or infinite. Submit: a document, worksheet, slide, image, or link that shows the completed work. Include: calculations, diagrams, equations, or graphs from the Introduction to Limits module, plus a short explanation of how your work supports your answer. Evaluation: the teacher should be able to see the final product, the steps or evidence used, and one clear connection to the module objective.

questions.question: If f(x) = 2x + 1 and g(x) = x², what is f(g(3))?
questions.explanation: g(3) = 9. f(9) = 2(9) + 1 = 19. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Pre-Calculus midterm concepts.

questions.question: Which of the following is the inverse of f(x) = (x − 3)/2?
questions.explanation: Swap x and y: x = (y − 3)/2 → 2x = y − 3 → y = 2x + 3. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Pre-Calculus midterm concepts, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

questions.question: A rational function has a hole at x = 2. This means the factor (x − 2) appears in:
questions.explanation: A hole (removable discontinuity) occurs when a common factor cancels from both numerator and denominator.

questions.question: In Pre-Calculus midterm concepts, Solve for x: log₂(x) = 5.
questions.explanation: log₂(x) = 5 means x = 2^5 = 32. Students need to recognize why x = 32 matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: In a right triangle, if the angle θ = 30° and the hypotenuse = 10, what is the side opposite to θ?
questions.explanation: sin 30° = opposite/hypotenuse = ½. Opposite = 10 × ½ = 5. This reinforces the Pre-Calculus midterm concepts idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

questions.question: In Pre-Calculus midterm concepts, Convert 270° to radians.
questions.explanation: 270° × (π/180°) = 270π/180 = 3π/2. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Pre-Calculus midterm concepts.

questions.question: What is the amplitude of y = −4 sin(2x + π)?
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 4.

questions.question: Which identity is a double-angle formula for cos(2θ)?
questions.explanation: One standard double-angle identity is cos(2θ) = cos²θ − sin²θ. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Pre-Calculus midterm concepts, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

questions.question: Solve sin(x) = √2/2 on [0, 2π).
questions.explanation: sin(x) = √2/2 at x = π/4 (Q1) and x = π − π/4 = 3π/4 (Q2). It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Pre-Calculus midterm concepts.

questions.question: A triangle has sides a = 7, b = 10, and angle A = 40°. Using the Law of Sines, which expression gives angle B?
questions.explanation: Law of Sines: a/sin A = b/sin B → sin B = b·sin A / a = (10 sin 40°)/7. It connects the correct answer to Pre-Calculus midterm concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: Given vectors u = ⟨3, −1⟩ and v = ⟨2, 4⟩, find u · v (dot product).
questions.explanation: u · v = (3)(2) + (−1)(4) = 6 − 4 = 2. This reinforces the Pre-Calculus midterm concepts idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

questions.question: What is the period of y = 3 cos(πx)?
questions.explanation: For y = a cos(bx), the period is 2π/|b|. Here b = π, so the period is 2. Students need to recognize why 2 matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: A function f has the graph shown. From the context, f is piecewise: f(x) = x + 2 for x < 1 and f(x) = 3 for x ≥ 1. State the domain, range, and evaluate f(0) + f(1).
questions.answer: Domain: all real numbers; Range: (−∞, 3] ∪ {3} = (−∞, 3]; f(0) + f(1) = 2 + 3 = 5.
questions.explanation: f(0) = 0 + 2 = 2 (using first piece). f(1) = 3 (using second piece). Sum = 5. Range: x < 1 gives (−∞, 3) from x + 2; x ≥ 1 gives {3}. Combined range: (−∞, 3].

questions.question: The loudness L (in decibels) of a sound is L = 10 log(I/I₀), where I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m². A sound has intensity I = 10⁻⁴ W/m². Find L.
questions.answer: L = 80 decibels. A strong response should use Pre-Calculus midterm concepts vocabulary, answer the full prompt, and include reasoning or evidence rather than a one-word reply.

questions.question: Verify the identity: (1 − cos²θ)/sinθ = sinθ.
questions.answer: sin²θ/sinθ = sinθ. A strong response should use Pre-Calculus midterm concepts vocabulary, answer the full prompt, and include reasoning or evidence rather than a one-word reply.
questions.explanation: By the Pythagorean identity, 1 − cos²θ = sin²θ. So (sin²θ)/sinθ = sinθ (for sinθ ≠ 0).

questions.question: If f(x) = x² − 1 and g(x) = √x, what is the domain of g(f(x))?
questions.explanation: g(f(x)) = √(x² − 1), so x² − 1 must be nonnegative. Thus x ≤ −1 or x ≥ 1. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Pre-Calculus final concepts, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

questions.question: Which of the following polynomial end behaviors describes f(x) = −2x⁴ + 3x² − 1?
options: Up on left, up on right
Down on left, up on right
Down on left, down on right
Up on left, down on right
questions.answer: Down on left, down on right
questions.explanation: The leading term is −2x⁴ (negative, even degree), so f(x) → −∞ as x → ±∞. Students need to recognize why Down on left, down on right matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: In Pre-Calculus final concepts, Solve: 3^(2x−1) = 27.
questions.explanation: 27 = 3³, so 2x − 1 = 3 → 2x = 4 → x = 2. This reinforces the Pre-Calculus final concepts idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

questions.question: An observer 50 m from the base of a building measures the angle of elevation to the top as 65°. Which expression gives the building's height?
questions.explanation: tan 65° = height/50 → height = 50 tan 65°. Students need to recognize why 50 tan 65° matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

questions.question: Which option best completes this statement: The reference angle for 5π/6 is...
questions.explanation: The best answer is π/6. It directly fits the prompt, while the other choices reflect different course ideas, incomplete definitions, or common mistakes.

questions.question: The graph of y = 2sin(3x − π/2) + 1 has a phase shift of:
questions.explanation: Phase shift = (π/2)/3 = π/6. Since the form is sin(3(x − π/6)), the shift is π/6 to the right.

questions.question: Use the sum formula to find the exact value of sin(75°).
In triangle ABC, a = 8, b = 11, c = 6. Using the Law of Cosines, which expression gives cos A?
questions.explanation: Law of Cosines: a² = b² + c² − 2bc cos A → cos A = (b² + c² − a²)/(2bc) = (121 + 36 − 64)/(2 × 11 × 6).

questions.question: What is the exact component form of a vector with magnitude 5 and direction angle 53° from the positive x-axis?
questions.explanation: A vector with magnitude r and angle θ has component form ⟨r cos θ, r sin θ⟩. This is approximately ⟨3, 4⟩ for 53°.

questions.question: The parametric equations x = 2t, y = t² describe which curve?
questions.explanation: Eliminate t: t = x/2, so y = (x/2)² = x²/4. This is a parabola. It connects the correct answer to Pre-Calculus final concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

questions.question: Convert the polar point (4, π/3) to rectangular coordinates.
questions.explanation: x = 4cos(π/3) = 4(1/2) = 2. y = 4sin(π/3) = 4(√3/2) = 2√3. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Pre-Calculus final concepts.

questions.question: Which equation represents an ellipse with semi-major axis 5 along the x-axis and semi-minor axis 3?
questions.explanation: Standard ellipse: x²/a² + y²/b² = 1, a = 5 along x-axis, b = 3. So x²/25 + y²/9 = 1.

questions.question: The 5th term of the geometric sequence 3, 6, 12, 24, … is:
questions.explanation: The correct answer is: 48.

questions.question: How many ways can a committee of 3 be chosen from 8 people?
questions.explanation: C(8,3) = 8!/(3! × 5!) = (8 × 7 × 6)/(3 × 2 × 1) = 56. This reinforces the Pre-Calculus final concepts idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

questions.question: What is the coefficient of x³ in the expansion of (x + 2)⁵?
questions.explanation: Use the Binomial Theorem: the x³ term has k=2, giving C(5,2)·x³·2² = 10·4 = 40.

questions.question: Evaluate lim(x→3) (x² − 9)/(x − 3).
questions.explanation: Factor: (x²−9)/(x−3) = (x+3)(x−3)/(x−3) = x+3. As x→3, limit = 3+3 = 6. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Pre-Calculus final concepts, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

questions.question: Which statement is NOT required for f(x) to be continuous at x = a?
questions.explanation: Continuity requires the function value to exist, the limit to exist, and the limit to equal the function value; it does not require f(a) to be zero.

questions.question: Find the focus and directrix of the parabola y = (1/8)x².
questions.answer: Focus: (0, 2); Directrix: y = −2. A strong response should use Pre-Calculus final concepts vocabulary, answer the full prompt, and include reasoning or evidence rather than a one-word reply.
questions.explanation: Rewrite as x² = 8y. In form x² = 4py: 4p = 8 → p = 2. Focus is (0, p) = (0, 2) and directrix is y = −p = −2.

questions.question: Prove by mathematical induction that 1 + 2 + 3 + … + n = n(n+1)/2 for all positive integers n.
questions.answer: Base: n=1 → 1 = 1(2)/2 = 1 ✓. Inductive step: Assume true for n=k; show for n=k+1. Sum to k+1 = k(k+1)/2 + (k+1) = (k+1)(k/2 + 1) = (k+1)(k+2)/2 ✓.
questions.explanation: Base case verified. Inductive step: adding the (k+1)th term to the inductive hypothesis yields (k+1)(k+2)/2, which matches the formula for n = k+1.

questions.question: Describe the continuity of f(x) = (x² − 4)/(x − 2) at x = 2. Identify the type of discontinuity and redefine f(2) to make f continuous.
questions.answer: Removable discontinuity at x = 2. Redefine f(2) = 4. A strong response should use Pre-Calculus final concepts vocabulary, answer the full prompt, and include reasoning or evidence rather than a one-word reply.
questions.explanation: f(x) = (x−2)(x+2)/(x−2) = x+2 for x ≠ 2. The limit as x→2 is 4, but f(2) is undefined. This is a removable discontinuity. Setting f(2) = 4 makes f continuous everywhere.

quizQuestions.question: If f(x) = 3x − 2 and g(x) = x + 5, what is (f ∘ g)(x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: f(g(x)) = f(x+5) = 3(x+5) − 2 = 3x + 15 − 2 = 3x + 13. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Functions: Review & Advanced Concepts, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following functions does NOT have an inverse that is also a function?
quizQuestions.explanation: f(x) = x² fails the horizontal line test over all reals, so its inverse is not a function.

quizQuestions.question: The domain of f(x) = √(x − 4) is:
quizQuestions.explanation: The radicand must be non-negative: x − 4 ≥ 0 → x ≥ 4. It connects the correct answer to Functions: Review & Advanced Concepts and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: Compute the composition (f ∘ g)(x) for f(x) = 3x − 2 and g(x) = x + 5.
quizQuestions.explanation: f(g(x)) = f(x + 5) = 3(x + 5) − 2 = 3x + 13. This reinforces the Functions: Review & Advanced Concepts idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Complete this statement: A piecewise function is defined as f(x) = x + 1 for x < 2 and f(x) = x² for x ≥ 2. what is f(2) + f(0)...
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "5" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: What does the end behavior of f(x) = 5x³ − 2x + 1 look like?
options: Down on left, up on right
Up on left, down on right
quizQuestions.answer: Down on left, up on right
quizQuestions.explanation: Odd degree with positive leading coefficient: falls to −∞ on left, rises to +∞ on right.

quizQuestions.question: How many real zeros can a polynomial of degree 4 have at most?
quizQuestions.explanation: By the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra, a degree-4 polynomial has at most 4 real zeros.

quizQuestions.question: The rational function f(x) = (x − 3)/((x − 3)(x + 2)) has:
options: A vertical asymptote at x = 3 and x = −2
A hole at x = 3 and a vertical asymptote at x = −2
A hole at x = −2 and a vertical asymptote at x = 3
quizQuestions.answer: A hole at x = 3 and a vertical asymptote at x = −2
quizQuestions.explanation: The factor (x − 3) cancels, creating a hole at x = 3. The factor (x + 2) remains in the denominator, giving a vertical asymptote at x = −2.

quizQuestions.question: For f(x) = 1/(x + 4), what is the horizontal asymptote?
quizQuestions.explanation: Degree of numerator (0) is less than degree of denominator (1), so the horizontal asymptote is y = 0.

quizQuestions.question: What is the end behavior of f(x) = 5x³ − 2x + 1?
options: Up on left, up on right
Down on left, down on right
Down on left, up on right
Up on left, down on right
quizQuestions.answer: Down on left, up on right
quizQuestions.explanation: A cubic with a positive leading coefficient falls to the left and rises to the right.

quizQuestions.question: In Exponential & Logarithmic Functions, Simplify: log₃(27).
quizQuestions.explanation: log₃(27) = log₃(3³) = 3. This reinforces the Exponential & Logarithmic Functions idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Which property allows you to write log(AB) as log A + log B?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Product Rule: logₐ(MN) = logₐ M + logₐ N. Students need to recognize why Product Rule matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: Solve: e^x = 15. Which expression gives the exact answer?
quizQuestions.explanation: Taking the natural log of both sides: ln(eˣ) = ln 15 → x = ln 15. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Exponential & Logarithmic Functions.

quizQuestions.question: In Exponential & Logarithmic Functions, Simplify log₃(27).
quizQuestions.explanation: Since 3^3 = 27, log₃(27) = 3. This reinforces the Exponential & Logarithmic Functions idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Which logarithm property allows you to write log(AB) as log A + log B?
quizQuestions.explanation: The product rule for logarithms says log(AB) = log A + log B. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Exponential & Logarithmic Functions.

quizQuestions.question: In a right triangle with legs 5 and 12, what is sin of the angle opposite the leg of length 5?
quizQuestions.explanation: Hypotenuse = √(25 + 144) = 13. sin = opposite/hypotenuse = 5/13. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Right Triangle Trigonometry.

quizQuestions.question: What is the exact value of tan(45°)?
quizQuestions.explanation: In a 45-45-90 triangle, both legs are equal, so tan(45°) = opposite/adjacent = 1.

quizQuestions.question: If cos θ = 5/13 in a right triangle, what is tan θ?
quizQuestions.explanation: cos θ = adjacent/hypotenuse = 5/13, so opposite = √(13² − 5²) = 12. tan θ = 12/5.

quizQuestions.question: The inverse trig function sin⁻¹(0.5) gives an angle of:
quizQuestions.explanation: sin(30°) = 0.5, so sin⁻¹(0.5) = 30°. This reinforces the Right Triangle Trigonometry idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Complete this statement: In a 30-60-90 triangle with hypotenuse 2, what is the side opposite the 60° angle...
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "√3" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Define or explain: The inverse trig function sin⁻¹(0.5) gives an angle of.
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "30°" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: Convert 5π/4 radians to degrees.
quizQuestions.explanation: 5π/4 × (180/π) = 5 × 45 = 225°. It connects the correct answer to Unit Circle & Radian Measure and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: What is the reference angle for 7π/6?
quizQuestions.explanation: 7π/6 is in Q3. Reference angle = 7π/6 − π = π/6. It connects the correct answer to Unit Circle & Radian Measure and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: On the unit circle, what are the coordinates at angle π/3?
quizQuestions.explanation: At π/3 (60°): cos(60°) = 1/2, sin(60°) = √3/2. Coordinates: (1/2, √3/2). The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Unit Circle & Radian Measure.

quizQuestions.question: In Unit Circle & Radian Measure, Arc length formula is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Arc length: s = rθ, where θ must be in radians and r is the radius. It connects the correct answer to Unit Circle & Radian Measure and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: In which quadrant is sin negative and cos positive?
quizQuestions.explanation: In Q4: x > 0 (cos positive), y < 0 (sin negative). The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Unit Circle & Radian Measure, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

quizQuestions.question: What degree measure is equivalent to 5π/4 radians?
quizQuestions.explanation: Multiply by 180°/π: (5π/4)(180°/π) = 225°. Students need to recognize why 225° matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: What is the period of y = sin(4x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Period = 2π/|B| = 2π/4 = π/2. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Graphs of Trig Functions, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

quizQuestions.question: What is the amplitude of y = −5 cos(x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Amplitude = |−5| = 5. It is always a positive value. It connects the correct answer to Graphs of Trig Functions and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: The graph of y = sin(x − π/4) is the graph of y = sin(x) shifted:
quizQuestions.explanation: Replacing x with (x − π/4) shifts the graph π/4 units to the right. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Graphs of Trig Functions.

quizQuestions.question: Which calculation correctly finds the period of y = sin(4x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: For y = sin(Bx), the period is 2π / |B|. Here B = 4, so 2π / 4 simplifies to π/2; multiplying by 4 instead would confuse frequency with period.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a Pythagorean identity?
quizQuestions.explanation: The fundamental Pythagorean identity is sin²θ + cos²θ = 1. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Trig Identities & Equations.

quizQuestions.question: Simplify: sin²x/(1 − cos²x).
quizQuestions.explanation: 1 − cos²x = sin²x by Pythagorean identity. So sin²x/sin²x = 1. It connects the correct answer to Trig Identities & Equations and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: In Trig Identities & Equations, what is sin(A + B)?
quizQuestions.explanation: Sum formula: sin(A + B) = sinA cosB + cosA sinB. This reinforces the Trig Identities & Equations idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Select the fundamental Pythagorean identity for sine and cosine.
quizQuestions.explanation: The fundamental Pythagorean identity is sin²θ + cos²θ = 1. It connects the correct answer to Trig Identities & Equations and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Define or explain: The double-angle formula sin(2θ) equals.
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "2sinθcosθ" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: Which law should you use to solve a triangle when given two sides and the angle between them (SAS)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Law of Cosines is used for SAS and SSS cases in oblique triangles. Students need to recognize why Law of Cosines matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: In the ambiguous case (SSA), how many triangles exist if the given side opposite the angle is greater than the other given side?
quizQuestions.explanation: When a > b (the side opposite the given angle is larger than the other given side), exactly one triangle is formed.

quizQuestions.question: The area of a triangle with sides a = 6, b = 8, and included angle C = 30° is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Area = ½ab sin C = ½(6)(8) sin 30° = ½(48)(0.5) = 12. This reinforces the Law of Sines & Cosines idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Law of Cosines: c² = a² + b² − 2ab cos C. If C = 90°, this reduces to:
quizQuestions.explanation: cos(90°) = 0, so 2ab cos(90°) = 0. The formula becomes c² = a² + b² — the Pythagorean Theorem.

quizQuestions.question: Which law should you use to solve a triangle when given two sides and the included angle (SAS)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The Law of Cosines is used for SAS because it relates two sides and the included angle to the third side.

quizQuestions.question: What is the magnitude of vector v = ⟨−3, 4⟩?
quizQuestions.explanation: |v| = √((-3)² + 4²) = √(9 + 16) = √25 = 5. It connects the correct answer to Vectors and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: Two vectors u and v are orthogonal if:
quizQuestions.explanation: Vectors are orthogonal (perpendicular) if and only if their dot product equals zero.

quizQuestions.question: Find the dot product of u = ⟨2, 5⟩ and v = ⟨−1, 3⟩.
quizQuestions.explanation: u · v = (2)(−1) + (5)(3) = −2 + 15 = 13. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Vectors.

quizQuestions.question: Which calculation correctly finds the magnitude of vector v = ⟨−3, 4⟩?
quizQuestions.explanation: Vector magnitude uses the distance formula. Squaring both components gives 9 + 16 = 25, and the square root is 5.

quizQuestions.question: Two vectors u and v are orthogonal if which condition is true?
quizQuestions.explanation: Orthogonal vectors have a dot product of zero. It connects the correct answer to Vectors and separates it from plausible distractors.

quizQuestions.question: The parametric equations x = cos t, y = sin t for t ∈ [0, 2π) describe:
quizQuestions.explanation: x² + y² = cos²t + sin²t = 1. This is the equation of a unit circle. This reinforces the Parametric Equations & Polar Coordinates idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: Convert the rectangular point (−1, √3) to polar coordinates (r, θ).
quizQuestions.explanation: r = √(1 + 3) = 2. θ = arctan(√3/−1); since the point is in Q2, θ = π − π/3 = 2π/3.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the polar equation of a circle of radius 5 centered at the origin?
quizQuestions.explanation: A circle of radius 5 centered at the origin has the simple polar equation r = 5.

quizQuestions.question: Eliminate the parameter from x = t + 1, y = 2t − 3 to get the rectangular equation.
quizQuestions.explanation: t = x − 1. Substitute: y = 2(x − 1) − 3 = 2x − 2 − 3 = 2x − 5. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Parametric Equations & Polar Coordinates.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Define or explain: The polar equation r = 1 − cos θ represents which type of curve.
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "A cardioid" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: The parabola x² = 12y opens:
quizQuestions.explanation: The form x² = 4py with p > 0 opens upward. Here 4p = 12 → p = 3 > 0. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Conic Sections (Advanced).

quizQuestions.question: For the ellipse x²/16 + y²/9 = 1, the length of the major axis is:
quizQuestions.explanation: a² = 16 → a = 4. Major axis length = 2a = 8. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Conic Sections (Advanced).

quizQuestions.question: What is the eccentricity of a circle?
quizQuestions.explanation: A circle has eccentricity e = 0. Ellipses: 0 < e < 1. Parabolas: e = 1. Hyperbolas: e > 1.

quizQuestions.question: Which conic section has two branches?
quizQuestions.explanation: A hyperbola consists of two separate branches opening in opposite directions.

quizQuestions.question: Completing the square in x² + 6x + y² − 4y = 12 yields a circle with center:
quizQuestions.explanation: (x+3)² + (y−2)² = 12 + 9 + 4 = 25. Center: (−3, 2), radius: 5. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Conic Sections (Advanced).

quizQuestions.question: The parabola x² = 12y opens in which direction?
quizQuestions.explanation: The equation x² = 4py has a vertical axis. Since 4p = 12 is positive, it opens upward.

quizQuestions.question: Find the common difference of the arithmetic sequence: 7, 11, 15, 19, …
quizQuestions.explanation: Common difference d = 11 − 7 = 4. The reasoning points back to the procedure used in Sequences, Series & Mathematical Induction, which helps students check the calculation rather than guess from the options.

quizQuestions.question: What is the 10th term of the geometric sequence 2, 6, 18, …?
quizQuestions.explanation: a₁ = 2, r = 3. aₙ = 2 × 3^(n−1). a₁₀ = 2 × 3⁹. This reinforces the Sequences, Series & Mathematical Induction idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: The sum of the first n terms of an arithmetic series with first term a₁ and last term aₙ is:
quizQuestions.explanation: Arithmetic series sum: Sₙ = n(a₁ + aₙ)/2. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Sequences, Series & Mathematical Induction.

quizQuestions.question: Find the common difference of the arithmetic sequence 7, 11, 15, 19, ...
quizQuestions.explanation: Each term increases by 4, so the common difference is 4. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Sequences, Series & Mathematical Induction.

quizQuestions.question: Short response: Complete this statement: A geometric series has first term 5 and common ratio 1/2. the sum of the infinite series is...
quizQuestions.explanation: A correct response identifies "10" and connects it to the module context.

quizQuestions.question: How many ways can 5 books be arranged on a shelf?
quizQuestions.explanation: 5! = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120 (permutations of 5 distinct items). This reinforces the Probability & Combinatorics (Advanced) idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: How many ways can a president and vice president be chosen from a club of 10 members?
quizQuestions.explanation: Order matters (positions are distinct): P(10, 2) = 10 × 9 = 90. This reinforces the Probability & Combinatorics (Advanced) idea behind the item instead of rewarding a surface-level guess.

quizQuestions.question: In Probability & Combinatorics (Advanced), what is C(7, 3)?
quizQuestions.explanation: C(7,3) = 7!/(3! × 4!) = (7 × 6 × 5)/(3 × 2 × 1) = 210/6 = 35. It highlights the operation, property, or model that makes the answer work in Probability & Combinatorics (Advanced).

quizQuestions.question: How many ways can 5 distinct books be arranged on a shelf?
quizQuestions.explanation: There are 5! arrangements, and 5! = 120. Students need to recognize why 120 matches the evidence in the question and why nearby options do not.

quizQuestions.question: What does lim(x→2) f(x) = 5 mean?
options: f approaches 5 as x approaches 2 from the right only
f(x) gets arbitrarily close to 5 as x gets close to 2 from either side
quizQuestions.answer: f(x) gets arbitrarily close to 5 as x gets close to 2 from either side
quizQuestions.explanation: A limit describes the behavior of f(x) as x approaches a value, not necessarily the function value at that point.

quizQuestions.question: Evaluate: lim(x→4) (2x + 1).
quizQuestions.explanation: By direct substitution (polynomial): 2(4) + 1 = 9. The choice fits the prompt because it applies the central rule or concept from Introduction to Limits.

quizQuestions.question: A function f has lim(x→3⁻) f(x) = 4 and lim(x→3⁺) f(x) = 7. What is lim(x→3) f(x)?
quizQuestions.explanation: The two-sided limit exists only if both one-sided limits are equal. Since 4 ≠ 7, the limit does not exist.

quizQuestions.question: What type of discontinuity does f(x) = 1/x have at x = 0?
quizQuestions.explanation: As x → 0, f(x) = 1/x → ±∞. This is an infinite (or essential) discontinuity.

quizQuestions.question: Interpret the two-sided limit statement lim(x→2) f(x) = 5.
options: f(x) gets arbitrarily close to 5 as x gets close to 2 from either side
f(x) gets arbitrarily close to 2 as x gets close to 5
The function must be linear near x = 2
quizQuestions.answer: f(x) gets arbitrarily close to 5 as x gets close to 2 from either side
quizQuestions.explanation: A two-sided limit describes the value f(x) approaches as x approaches 2 from both sides.
